chapter_info.subject: Social Science - Civics
chapter_info.chapter_name: What is Democracy? Why is Democracy?

flashcards.q: What is the simple definition of democracy?
flashcards.a: A form of government in which the rulers are elected by the people.

flashcards.q: Is the simple definition of democracy sufficient? Why or why not?
flashcards.a: No; it helps identify democracies but is incomplete. Elections can exist without genuine choice or real power transfers.

flashcards.q: Name the first feature of a truly democratic government.
flashcards.a: Rulers must be elected by the people and final decision-making power must rest with elected representatives.

flashcards.q: Name the second feature of a truly democratic government.
flashcards.a: Elections must be free and fair, offering real choice between alternatives with genuine opportunity to remove current rulers.

flashcards.q: Name the third feature of a truly democratic government.
flashcards.a: This choice and voting rights must be available to all people on an equal basis (one person, one vote, one value).

flashcards.q: Name the fourth feature of a truly democratic government.
flashcards.a: Government must rule within limits set by constitutional law and citizens' rights; even elected governments are limited by law.

flashcards.q: What does 'rule of law' mean?
flashcards.a: No person, including rulers and government officials, is above the law; all are subject to the same laws.

flashcards.q: Why is having elected rulers important in democracy?
flashcards.a: It ensures accountability; if voters are dissatisfied with performance, they can elect different representatives who will make different decisions.

flashcards.q: What makes elections 'free'?
flashcards.a: People vote without fear or coercion; voters are able to vote according to their true preferences without intimidation.

flashcards.q: What makes elections 'fair'?
flashcards.a: All parties can campaign freely, media covers all parties, opposition has realistic chance of winning, and votes are counted honestly.

flashcards.q: What is the principle of 'one person, one vote, one value'?
flashcards.a: Every adult citizen has equal voting rights, and each vote counts equally regardless of wealth, education, caste, religion, or gender.

flashcards.q: What are constitutional rights?
flashcards.a: Fundamental freedoms guaranteed to citizens that government cannot violate, even if elected; they protect minorities and individual liberty.

flashcards.q: Why are constitutional rights necessary in democracy?
flashcards.a: To prevent tyranny of majority; even if elected government and majority want to oppress minorities, constitutional rights protect them.

flashcards.q: What happened to Pakistan under General Musharraf regarding democracy?
flashcards.a: Though elections occurred and Parliament existed, real power rested with Musharraf (not elected) who could dismiss Parliament, making it undemocratic.

flashcards.q: Why wasn't China's electoral system democratic despite holding elections?
flashcards.a: Only communist party members and allies could contest; opposition was forbidden, so real choice was absent.

flashcards.q: Why wasn't Mexico's electoral system democratic before 2000 despite opposition parties existing?
flashcards.a: Same party (PRI) won for 71 years through media control, government coercion, and manipulation, making opposition victory impossible despite theoretical competition.

flashcards.q: What case demonstrates that elections alone do not ensure democracy?
flashcards.a: Zimbabwe under Mugabe; Mugabe was elected but violated constitutional rights, suppressed opposition, and ignored courts.

flashcards.q: What is political equality?
flashcards.a: All citizens have equal status in governance regardless of wealth, education, social status, or birth; equal voice in political decisions.

flashcards.q: What is the strongest argument for democracy?
flashcards.a: That democracy enhances human dignity by treating all people as equal and capable of self-governance, not mere subjects of rulers.

flashcards.q: How does democracy respond to people's needs better than non-democracy?
flashcards.a: Democratic governments must be responsive or face electoral defeat; non-democratic governments have no such pressure to serve people's needs.

flashcards.q: What does the India-China famine comparison reveal about democracy?
flashcards.a: India prevented large-scale famine through government responsiveness to food shortages; non-democratic China's government ignored famine killing 3 crore, showing democracy's accountability.

flashcards.q: Why was India not devastated by famine like China despite similar economic conditions?
flashcards.a: Democratic India had media reporting, opposition demanding action, and government facing electoral threat if non-responsive, creating pressure for action.

flashcards.q: How does democratic consultation improve decision-making quality?
flashcards.a: Multiple people examining decisions identify more mistakes and unintended consequences than single dictator; quality improvement justifies time cost.

flashcards.q: Why is democracy important for handling diversity in a country like India?
flashcards.a: Diverse groups (religions, languages, castes, regions) can compete peacefully; no group is permanently excluded or dominant, allowing coexistence without violent conflict.

flashcards.q: What happens in non-democratic systems when groups have different interests?
flashcards.a: Powerful groups impose terms on weaker ones by force, creating resentment and instability without mechanism for peaceful resolution.

flashcards.q: How can democracy correct its own mistakes?
flashcards.a: Through public discussion pointing out errors, opposition criticism, electoral removal of bad rulers, and law reforms.

flashcards.q: Can non-democratic systems correct mistakes easily?
flashcards.a: No; once rulers make mistakes, there is no peaceful mechanism to correct or remove them, making errors potentially permanent and catastrophic.

flashcards.q: What does it mean when democracies are called 'self-correcting'?
flashcards.a: Democracies have mechanisms to identify and fix errors through elections, public discussion, and law changes; this capacity makes democracy safer than alternatives.

flashcards.q: Can democracy exist beyond government?
flashcards.a: Yes; democracy as a principle of consultation and equal voice can apply to families, schools, workplaces, and organizations.

flashcards.q: What makes a family 'democratic'?
flashcards.a: When parents seek children's opinions before decisions and children's voices matter equally to parents' in decision-making.

flashcards.q: What makes a school 'democratic'?
flashcards.a: When teachers allow students to speak and ask questions, and student voices shape classroom governance and decisions.

flashcards.q: What is democracy as an 'ideal standard'?
flashcards.a: Not description of existing democracies, but aspirational goals all democracies should aim toward (eliminating poverty, ensuring equal education, equal opportunity).

flashcards.q: What is 'minimal democracy' vs. 'good democracy'?
flashcards.a: Minimal democracy has free elections and rule of law; good democracy additionally provides no poverty, equal education, and genuine economic opportunity.

flashcards.q: Does any country achieve 'good democracy' completely?
flashcards.a: No; all existing democracies fall short of ideal democracy in some ways but work toward realizing democratic ideals.

flashcards.q: Why does democracy require constant citizen effort and vigilance?
flashcards.a: Because no perfect democracy exists; citizens must continuously protect rights, demand accountability, and work toward expanding freedom and justice.

flashcards.q: What is the 'weakness and strength' of democracy according to the chapter?
flashcards.a: Weakness and strength: the fate of democracy depends not just on what rulers do but on what citizens do; requires active participation.

flashcards.q: What does 'tyranny of majority' mean?
flashcards.a: When elected majority uses power to oppress or deny rights to minorities, prevented by constitutional protections in true democracies.

flashcards.q: Why is it inaccurate to say 'democracy is rule by the majority'?
flashcards.a: Because true democracy limits majority power through constitutional rights, protecting minorities from oppression even when majority wishes it.

flashcards.q: Is it possible for a government to be elected but undemocratic?
flashcards.a: Yes; if government violates rule of law, suppresses rights, controls media, or uses violence against opposition, it is undemocratic despite being elected.

flashcards.q: What makes a government 'legitimate' in democracy?
flashcards.a: Acceptance by people as rightful and proper, achieved through free elections, respect for rights, and adherence to constitutional limits.

flashcards.q: Can a dictatorship hold elections and still be undemocratic?
flashcards.a: Yes; elections without real choice or with powerless elected representatives are not truly democratic.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between freedom and democracy?
flashcards.a: Democracy requires freedoms (speech, assembly, association) for genuine competition and choice; without freedoms, elections are not free.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between equality and democracy?
flashcards.a: Democracy is based on political equality; unequal voting power or restricted voting rights undermine democratic principle.

flashcards.q: Why can't democracy exist without rule of law?
flashcards.a: Without rule of law, rulers can violate rights without consequence; constitutional limits on power become meaningless.

flashcards.q: How do citizens strengthen democracy?
flashcards.a: By participating in elections, demanding accountability, protecting minority rights, engaging in public discussion, and remaining vigilant against power abuses.

flashcards.q: Is democracy only about choosing leaders through elections?
flashcards.a: No; democracy means much more—it includes how government exercises power, respects rights, and how broader society makes decisions.
